title: Beyond the Monster Mother
wikitext:
[]: Beyond the Monster Mother             Quote Post by Chuckman » Sat Mar 22, 2014 1:05 pm Last night I was reading Hero With a Thousand Faces , specifically the chapter on meeting the Goddess . This led me down a path of realization and I began considering the frequency of certain numbers in Eva. The number three is the most obvious; systems of three crop up everywhere, with a specific dynamic. Consider: 1. Three MAGI computers. 2. Three pilots. 3. Three people in the apartment. 4. Three main Evas. Immediately I spring to the concept of one who is three, the tripartite god/goddess. As I have argued previously Eva is rife with occult meanings and twisting of the judeo-christian iconography it appropriates into an occult system. Specifically, I have identified Lilith/Rei as a Mother Goddess figure encompassing both positive and negative aspects- Lilith as alternative Eve and Lilith as Tiamat/Echidna/Monster Mother. Campbell's analysis of this duality in the goddess figure in the monomyth turned me to this once again and I realized that my understanding of the Eva Working -the goings on behind the symbology and the plot, or "gross anatomy" of the series, if you will- was not yet complete. The tripartite goddess' aspects are the Maid (or Virgin), Matron (or Mother) and Crone. These represent the stages of female life as contextualized by a growth or transformation of sexual power. On another level they represent past, present, and future, and womanhood as enticing, comforting, and terrifying. Many triple goddesses are remembered today through the lens of masculinized syncretic pantheons; in the ancient world it was common for one culture not to deny but to rename and appropriate deities from the spiritual practices of their enemies. Especially in the Mediterranean world, where the occult lore that Anno draws on for western influences in his work originates, the mythology we have recorded today is a garbled game of telephone from thousands of years of oral tradition, condensed down to a relative few sources. I don't even need to discuss the Magi System- they are openly, obviously a triple goddess figure, representing three aspects of the same woman. Woman/Mother/Scientist = Maid/Matron/Crone. The Tripartite Goddess is most obvious as a presence in Eva in Rei, but she is present repeatedly and interaction between her various aspects and masculine forces or conflict between those aspects is a repeated theme in the series. Observe: Note that the roles are inconsistent. There's a certain symmetry to this- the aspects of the Goddess are both distinct figures and the same being at different times in her own existence. The Goddess is both three separate entities and one entity; this trinity concept should be familiar from Christianity, which appropriated it from other mystery cults. This represents a crude, intuitive understanding of godforms as beings that fold the fourth dimension through the fifth . Some of these identifications are obvious; Rei I is the Maid, Rei II the Mother, and Rei III the Crone. They are combined and become a divinity. The others are subtle. The interaction between Rei (all of them), Naoko and Ritsuko is fascinating. At the time of Rei I's death, Ritsuko is still a schoolgirl, and still has her brown hair, and is if not virtuous and innocent at least unmarked by experience, but already doomed to become her mother. Naoko is the embodiment of matronhood, but is slippning into the realm of the Crone; the sexual period of her life is ending. Rei I is the bearer of this uncomfortable truth. By slaying her and then herself, Naoko elevates Ritsuko to the position of Matron while cementing Rei I as the Crone- the soul in Unit 00. Asuka, Misato, and Rei (II) make up another triple goddess system, but an inverted and twisted one. Asuka is a Maid who despises the inevitable loss of her virginity and transition into Matronhood; she both craves status and adulthood and loathes the idea of becoming a mother. Misato is a mother figure to the pilots but she's immature and though it is unconsummated makes herself sexually available to her male "child", a breach of the ultimate taboo. Rei is young, a pubescent child on the edge of sexual flowering, but has all the halmarks of an old woman, living in clincal, hospital-like decrepitude, surviving on medications, and longing for the release of death. Like the Crone she is distinct from but also embodies the other aspects as a motherly, asexual creature of age and wisdom. I had discussed, or at least, mentioned, these before. What fascinated me was an aspect I'd missed- the three mothers of the pilots (Rei, of course, being her own mother) and the three primary Evas that drive the story. The three Evas are heavily identified with their resident souls, hence their mother goddess framework requires a discussion of these individuals that give them breath and the Evas themselves. Kyoko is identified as the Maid, and the life-giving spark of the youngest Eva. Motherhood absolutely destroys her, even as she becomes Mother to Asuka's Maid. She eschews an actual daughter (Motherhood) for a toy (Maidenhood); as the Asuka-Misato-Rei system is corrupted and a twisted version of its true self, so is this one. Yui is, most obviously, even ringingly, the Mother, the trait that defines her. Yui is a cypher in the series, purposely- she is defined not as herself but as a memory, existing from our perspective solely through the perceptions of men who adore her. Fuyutsuki mourns her virginity, pining for Yui as pure Maid, while Gendo, having made her Mother with his seed, despises this aspect and wishes to complete a ritual that will in effect make her his Mother by placing him in her womb within the Eva. Rei is again the Crone, dispensing wisdom, but her Eva (which is also her ) is the most feared, the most feral, the most dangerous; Mother as Tiamat, woman as monster that despises men. In the series, avatars of the various aspects are raped or violated repeatedly, and sexual imagery is a frequent motif; the anatomy of the Evas is disturbingly feminine, as if summoned from the mind of a deranged Georgia O'Keeffe. Consider the Sachiel battle, with the angel's jackhammering pile driver attack. The masculine Angels constantly try to subdue and penetrate the Evas, and several of the battles, especially Bardiel and Armisael, are metaphors for the physical pain and loss of agency inherent in rape. Rei is subject to constant sexual violence, beginning with Shinji's intrusion into her private space and groping, continuing through her violation by Armisael and culminating in Gendo's symbolic rape-as-mastery in EoE. The first is a fumbling, virginal event, as confusing to Shinji as it is merely annoying to Rei, tied to the idea of Maid. The second is a twisted vision of the sperm penetrating the egg. The third is Gendo violating her, not through the genitals but with his very Self(hand), leading to a violent reaction. She bites the hand that feeds her and takes on a war-aspect, becoming death. Asuka undergoes three trials, as well. She experiences three rapes, and three deaths. The first of these is her violation by Arael; her fist death is her suicide attempt by starvation, enacted in a bathroom. This is important- the bathroom in the apartment is the only place where Asuka has any privacy after she moves into the apartment. The bathroom where she finds her first death is blasted open, the privacy removed, her innocence destroyed by war and violence. Her second violation comes at the hands of the Mass Production Evas, creatures that are as terrifyingly masculine as Lilith is ovwhelmingly feminine. As toothed penises they represent a violent answer to the fear of vagina dentata. In their overwhelming numbers they represent sperm competing for an egg, but in a terrible inversion. Their licking and grasping and shoving of their phallic swords into Asuka-Kyoko is a violent, and disdainful gang rape, a ritual destruction of a virgin sacrifice to open the final act of the rite. Again, death finds her. The third violation comes in the kitchen in pre-Insturmentality. This event takes place not in Shinji's mind, but in Asuka's . The kitchen seen here is to Asuka as the hell train is to Shinji- her own personal purgatory, a place of scouring and a gentle hell. The hallmarks are all there. She is alone, which she fears more than anything, surrounded by symbols of her failed attempts at connection (she is in Misato's kitchen- despite her crowing about being able to live by herself and needing no one, she has no other place but the one she shared, and the coffee is not in a cup but a pot, implying sharing). The symbolic rape comes when Shinji forcefully enters and disrupts her hell, imposing his self and his bullshit problems on her. When she rejects him he replies with force in a symbolic rape murder that initiates her into apotheosis. Some other instances of rape imagery: The implied rape of Ritsuko as punishment. Misato's death by gut shot, identifying her with Rei's ascension. Note that following this, Misato's kiss, her first and only expression of physical affection towards him, leaves him bloody, linking it to a loss of virginity. The attempted rape-by-spear of of Yui-as-Eva. Fast forward to the ending of the rite. The end result is Shinji and Asuka on a beach. They have emerged from the ocean, as all life did. Each takes on aspects of the other. Shinji straddles her, taking on a feminine aspect, that of a woman in intercourse with a supine man, taking rather than receiving ; at the same time he enacts violence against her, crushing her throat (voice) with his hands (self) until she offers kindness (mirroring his mother's touch, motherly love and comfort) which ends his assault on her. The distinctions between them are no longer clear cut. The ritual is one of sexual violence. SEELE are the Illuminati, they are Them, They, the Conspiracy, the High Priesthood that has spent all of history erasing feminine identity and working to steal the aspect of life-giver from the mysteries of womanhood and transfer it to the male aspect, to identify man as seed and woman as vessel (see From Hell , Alan Moore) and to destroy femininity with sexual violence. In understanding its failure, we must consider a tripartite God, which also exists within Eva. The most immediately familiar expression of this notion is the Christian trinity, but that is useless here because it is not a true triple god. Rather, it is a ritualistic erasure of the importance of woman in the reproductive cycle. Child becomes Son, only Father retains his identity, and Mother, the secret consort of Jehova called Asherah, is murdered leaving only a Holy Ghost. Rather, we need to consider the presence of male characters in the story- the most prominent one is Shinji, followed by Gendo, Fuyutsuki, Kaji, Hyuga, Aoba, Toji, and Kensuke. The others are background characters, there only to serve a plot purpose, and receive no development (although the admiral or whatever he is that appears in Asuka Strikes! is important as the bearded, Father God-esque hypermasculine commander of a feet of giant metal war penises traversing the ocean, the sacred font of life that is Tiamat the monster mother, but I digress) Shinji and the two boys at the school represent the Boy, equivalent of the Maid, the unblooded virgin. Toji is the stoicism of inexperience and childish seriousness, Kensuke the eagerness to pass through the rite of passage (hence his fixation on militaria and war, the playthings of the adult man and the defining aspect of adult masculinity in a tribal society organized for territorial warfare). Shinji combines aspects of both. He is at times feral and warlike and passive, terrified of Girls and entranced by their softness and comfort, the memory of his lost mother mingled with the growing need to sow his seed. When Shinji is removed from the equation this system of three, he is immediately replaced by Hikari, who herself embodies the goddess totality- she's virginal and inexperienced, yet acts as a mother (replacing her own absent one) and dispenses wisdom and acts as Queen within the community of the classroom, connecting her to the Crone. Within the room itself she is Mother to Asuka's Maid and Rei's Crone, as well. Gendo, Fuyutsuki, and Kaji represent aspects of the Father. Kaji is the Warm Father, the Boy's buddy. He's a cool dad who does things prefaced by "don't tell Mom", who guides the transition into adulthood with advice, comfort, and shared experience. As a spy archetype he seethes with violence and virility. He's Shinji's dad in the truest sense, much like Misato is his mother. (It is important that Kaji dies, and Shinji symbolically takes virgin blood on his lip from Misato. This is a highly charged, Freudian moment where Shinji is truly initiated into the world of adulthood with a storm of sex and violence). He's the Adult Man as a warming companion who welcomes the Boy as a future colleague. He is the cool uncle that gives you a beer at New Years and pretends not to notice when you get into the old stack of playboys in his closet. Gendo is the Disciplinarian, the Cold Father, the aspect that the Boy resents, the aspect that he longs to kill to cement his possession of the mother, the figure that, through resentment and competition, promps the Boy to seize, rather than transition into, adulthood as an act of defiance. Fuyutsuki then is the Old Father. Distant, cold, emotionless, the aspect of adulthood that the Boy fears, being a transition from Father to Grandfather or Sage. He is the Father who wishes to change places, to steal the place of Boy and be young again, but is full of both regret and wisdom. SEELE themselves, embodied in Chairman Keel, are the Sage, the final aspect. Like many sages from myth, Keel is blind but has mystic sight and is a master of secrets. He is also King, a ruler, the power behind thrones. The goal of SEELE is to rewrite the entire dynamic, to create a unified being that is masculine without feminity, life without womb. This fails; womanhood is fluid like water and receives and transforms rather than is destroyed by masculine force. The seed of Adam implanted in Rei by Gendo does not overwhelm her. She seizes and gives it shape; the vessel wins over the seed. The end result is equilibrium. The new Adam cannot kill or assert over the new Lilith. Asuka who is now three-in-one achieves victory over him with kindness and births a new age in sight of the old goddess who has been sacrificed to herself. Asuka is ultimately the new Goddess. Repeatedly, the enemy attempted to debase, slay, and steal the sun-fire from her. Rei is the Goddess as Moon, mere reflection of the Father Sun. Asuka in the new world is life giver, world maker, the Sun as Mother, womanhood ascendant.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Sat Mar 22, 2014 2:40 pm Gotta be honest here, Chuckman. TL/DR. Also what I did read of it seemed like nothing but confirmation bias and a whole lot of round pegs in square holes. It's there in Rei and the magi, yes, but everything else feels like you're trying far too hard for no real gain.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Sat Mar 22, 2014 5:30 pm The roles assigned to the Evangelions match the ones I used elsewhere --it's pretty much forced if you apply that template.

[]:             Quote Post by dereliction_carnival » Sun Mar 23, 2014 1:35 pm Don't be put off. Keep writing essays and stuff, even if nobody reads them :)

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Sun Mar 23, 2014 3:07 pm It's not just the rambling, disconnected form the essay takes that's the problem. Even if for the sake of argument I grant that it's all correct and this maid/mother/crone stuff is inherently present all over the place in eva... so what? How does this hypothetical information add to our understanding? In what way does any of this actually matter? That is what you've got to establish before I even really CARE if it's true.

[]:             Quote Post by dereliction_carnival » Tue Mar 25, 2014 12:47 pm A lot of the time an essay is a dialogue with oneself, thinking stuff out. It doesn't always have to be the debate team or shouting stuff through a megaphone. It's okay for someone to merely express what is in their heads! Saying this or that is a correct format becomes the opposite of exploration and kind of, well... fascist? If you want to play the right and wrong game, I think that as a mod you should change your profile picture to not be someone pointing a gun. How is that welcoming for new members, metaphorically speaking?

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Tue Mar 25, 2014 1:09 pm Because most people know that an internet avatar isn't at all indicative of how someone regards total internet strangers? On-Topic: Your essay is very interesting, and I don't mean this as a criticism of your past works, but this was the easiest to parse and understand, and is the most down-to-earth of them. I can't see anything I really disagree with. Regardless, I always love reading your material, even if I think you're batshit insane. Never stop being you, Chuckman.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Tue Mar 25, 2014 1:46 pm AuraTwilight wrote: On-Topic: Your essay is very interesting, and I don't mean this as a criticism of your past works, but this was the easiest to parse and understand, and is the most down-to-earth of them. I can't see anything I really disagree with.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Tue Mar 25, 2014 4:39 pm I'm with NemZ here; my reaction to all of it is "so what?" You're saying these patterns are there, but you aren't saying how or why they're relevant. I also think your approach is a little neat and tidy -- you have Asuka, Kyoko, and Unit 02 all playing the role of Maiden in their respective triads, but you missed (or ignored) the fact that these three are a triad of their own (same with Shinji, Yui, and Unit 01 and Unit 00, Rei II, and Lilith)). This approach also overlooks the fact that Misato has some of the cardinal traits of the Maiden, and that she serves in that role as well as that of Matron. I think it would be more interesting if you first noted the concrete examples of the triad as expressed in the show (the Magi and the iterations of Rei) and then looked at how the show actively subverted these ideas -- the complexity of Misato, the Asuka/Kyoko/Nigoki example, Asuka playing the role of hotheaded robot jock even though she's also a Maiden, etc. There's a lot to be mined there re: tropes and expectations and subversions and such, but you've stopped at the surface. Go deeper. You have the knowledge and the skill, and from this essay it seems you also have the motivation. Make it happen!

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Tue Mar 25, 2014 9:30 pm Bagheera wrote: I'm with NemZ here; my reaction to all of it is "so what?" You're saying these patterns are there, but you aren't saying how or why they're relevant. I also think your approach is a little neat and tidy -- you have Asuka, Kyoko, and Unit 02 all playing the role of Maiden in their respective triads, but you missed (or ignored) the fact that these three are a triad of their own (same with Shinji, Yui, and Unit 01 and Unit 00, Rei II, and Lilith)). This approach also overlooks the fact that Misato has some of the cardinal traits of the Maiden, and that she serves in that role as well as that of Matron. I think it would be more interesting if you first noted the concrete examples of the triad as expressed in the show (the Magi and the iterations of Rei) and then looked at how the show actively subverted these ideas -- the complexity of Misato, the Asuka/Kyoko/Nigoki example, Asuka playing the role of hotheaded robot jock even though she's also a Maiden, etc. There's a lot to be mined there re: tropes and expectations and subversions and such, but you've stopped at the surface. Go deeper. You have the knowledge and the skill, and from this essay it seems you also have the motivation. Make it happen!

[]:             Quote Post by mehdi_saada » Fri Oct 23, 2015 5:43 pm Warning, here's a constructivist opinion. I really liked it, even if the analogies are forced some times. SEELE is more a representation as much as the embodiment of the system of thoughts and beliefs we live in. Like the Matrice. You see beyond, you get smited instantly. Maybe Lorrenz is the Architect ? He didn't create the system, but is his current mastermind however. Because of that, they are more than conscious that humanity as it lives, is doomed. But they think it can't live otherwise(the system is hell-bent on conserving itself), so the only way out is suicide. Suicide is seen as transcendant, because the pain is escaped, but it is not the pain inherent to life, it is the pain to change your way of life, to change that system that makes life difficult. Which is exactly what Shinji and Asuka do in the film : the first faces his own lust and dependency, and choose to kill it and live. The first thing he does is killing the object of his lust. Asuka then shows him true tenderness, sign that chosed to go beyond the role males sticked on her before : the strong sexually enticing babe. Her statement of morning sickness would be a proof, that she saw how disgusting the gender/sexual system she lived was. Welcome in your trip from Campbell to W.Reich/Malinowsky Next, the rape of Asuka in EPE is in a sense justified, because the feminity she expressed is a false one built by lustful men( otakus ! ) to turn themselves on. Let's compare her to Nausicäa. Nausicäa would be much more like a Warrior Virgin( Athena ? ). You can say otakus would want deep inside to humiliate Asuka, because they feel the lie(they made it themselves ...), and they can also that way satisfy while feeling much less guilty to degrade a girl, their aggressive secondary pulsions on her. Only fine from a pulsion economical point of view : you create an object of hatred to have the liberty to use it the worst way possible.

[]:             Quote Post by unz » Sat Oct 24, 2015 12:29 am NemZ wrote: Gotta be honest here, Chuckman. TL/DR. Also what I did read of it seemed like nothing but confirmation bias and a whole lot of round blabla

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Sat Oct 24, 2015 12:37 am Yes, seriously. The triple goddess concept is used in the show but not nearly to the extent Chuckman is trying to force it into, which results in a whole lot of useless effort.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Sat Oct 24, 2015 12:48 am NemZ wrote: which results in a whole lot of useless effort.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Sat Oct 24, 2015 1:25 am You say that, and it sounds cool, but when I look at the post all I see are walls covered in photos and colored strings running everywhere, trying to cement so many tenuous connections at once that it runs together into a manic Freudian blur. Sometimes a cigar is just a cigar, man, so lay off the cocaine.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Sat Oct 24, 2015 2:08 am Also, we dealt with all of that a year and a half ago, so no need to rehash it all now.

[]:             Quote Post by mehdi_saada » Sat Oct 24, 2015 5:04 am why not ? As long as you have new ideas, why not go for it ? I resume the some psychanalytical categorizations. I think Eva don't have feminine traits, as they do not represent persons. they represent the pilots' partial pulsions. That is, the sexual pulsions in themselves, regardless to what they are used or invested in. Asuka sublimates hers in the quest for social acknowledgement(by showing off an ultra-sexuated obnoxious attitude), Shinji in the end, pilot Eva to be loved by his parents, which is less egoist, and after to save everyone, attitude that makes possible total identification with his pulsions , and the Imago of the Mother, and last Rei, which is the obediant little girl, doing everything because she thinks the Father is All-knowing and All-Mighty, the only provider of Love. Rei seems to an oeidipal counter-part of Shinji : he seeks love of his Mother, Rei the Father. The problem is, the Father often seeks to use the Little Girl to substitute for the love of his Wife, a love that disappeared quickly with marriage and 'conjugal duties'. Eva is such an easy representation of psychanalytic scenarii, what a beauty. But it goes beyond them.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Sat Oct 24, 2015 6:52 am mehdi_saada wrote: why not ? As long as you have new ideas, why not go for it ?

[]:             Quote Post by Dima » Sun Oct 25, 2015 8:11 am Good writing Chuckman. This was an interesting read but i think (like others pointed out) that some things are forced. Aside from the fact that there are 4 pilots (Toji) and 4 Evas (Eva-03) you could say that they don't count because their presence was very small. You say Asuka goes three trials and the third one is a symbolic rape in the kitchen in pre-Insturmentality. Why don't you consider the end of evangelion (Asuka and Shinji on the beach) as a forth trial? In my opinion it's more important and the ''hallmarks'' are all there too like in the previous cases that you mentioned. These are some of the examples that feel a bit forced. While there are more than three of the ''same'' situations in the show you decide to leave them out in order to prove that there is this ''concept of three'' that it may exist in some way but certainly not in a way to say that there is this strong symbolism in the show.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Mon Oct 26, 2015 1:44 pm Dima wrote: Good writing Chuckman. This was an interesting read but i think (like others pointed out) that some things are forced. Aside from the fact that there are 4 pilots (Toji) and 4 Evas (Eva-03) you could say that they don't count because their presence was very small.

Article: Beyond the Monster Mother
[]: Beyond the Monster Mother             Quote Post by Chuckman » Sat Mar 22, 2014 1:05 pm Last night I was reading Hero With a Thousand Faces , specifically the chapter on meeting the Goddess . This led me down a path of realization and I began considering the frequency of certain numbers in Eva. The number three is the most obvious; systems of three crop up everywhere, with a specific dynamic. Consider: 1. Three MAGI computers. 2. Three pilots. 3. Three people in the apartment. 4. Three main Evas. Immediately I spring to the concept of one who is three, the tripartite god/goddess. As I have argued previously Eva is rife with occult meanings and twisting of the judeo-christian iconography it appropriates into an occult system. Specifically, I have identified Lilith/Rei as a Mother Goddess figure encompassing both positive and negative aspects- Lilith as alternative Eve and Lilith as Tiamat/Echidna/Monster Mother. Campbell's analysis of this duality in the goddess figure in the monomyth turned me to this once again and I realized that my understanding of the Eva Working -the goings on behind the symbology and the plot, or "gross anatomy" of the series, if you will- was not yet complete. The tripartite goddess' aspects are the Maid (or Virgin), Matron (or Mother) and Crone. These represent the stages of female life as contextualized by a growth or transformation of sexual power. On another level they represent past, present, and future, and womanhood as enticing, comforting, and terrifying. Many triple goddesses are remembered today through the lens of masculinized syncretic pantheons; in the ancient world it was common for one culture not to deny but to rename and appropriate deities from the spiritual practices of their enemies. Especially in the Mediterranean world, where the occult lore that Anno draws on for western influences in his work originates, the mythology we have recorded today is a garbled game of telephone from thousands of years of oral tradition, condensed down to a relative few sources. Article: Beyond the Monster Mother

The Tripartite Goddess is most obvious as a presence in Eva in Rei, but she is present repeatedly and interaction between her various aspects and masculine forces or conflict between those aspects is a repeated theme in the series. Observe: Note that the roles are inconsistent. There's a certain symmetry to this- the aspects of the Goddess are both distinct figures and the same being at different times in her own existence. The Goddess is both three separate entities and one entity; this trinity concept should be familiar from Christianity, which appropriated it from other mystery cults. This represents a crude, intuitive understanding of godforms as beings that fold the fourth dimension through the fifth . Some of these identifications are obvious; Rei I is the Maid, Rei II the Mother, and Rei III the Crone. They are combined and become a divinity. The others are subtle. The interaction between Rei (all of them), Naoko and Ritsuko is fascinating. At the time of Rei I's death, Ritsuko is still a schoolgirl, and still has her brown hair, and is if not virtuous and innocent at least unmarked by experience, but already doomed to become her mother. By slaying her and then herself, Naoko elevates Ritsuko to the position of Matron while cementing Rei I as the Crone- the soul in Unit 00. Like the Crone she is distinct from but also embodies the other aspects as a motherly, asexual creature of age and wisdom. I had discussed, or at least, mentioned, these before. What fascinated me was an aspect I'd missed- the three mothers of the pilots (Rei, of course, being her own mother) and the three primary Evas that drive the story. The three Evas are heavily identified with their resident souls, hence their mother goddess framework requires a discussion of these individuals that give them breath and the Evas themselves. Kyoko is identified as the Maid, and the life-giving spark of the youngest Eva. Motherhood absolutely destroys her, even as she becomes Mother to Asuka's Maid. She eschews an actual daughter (Motherhood) for a toy (Maidenhood); as the Asuka-Misato-Rei system is corrupted and a twisted version of its true self, so is this one. Yui is, most obviously, even ringingly, the Mother, the trait that defines her. Yui is a cypher in the series, purposely- she is defined not as herself but as a memory, existing from our perspective solely through the perceptions of men who adore her. Fuyutsuki mourns her virginity, pining for Yui as pure Maid, while Gendo, having made her Mother with his seed, despises this aspect and wishes to complete a ritual that will in effect make her his Mother by placing him in her womb within the Eva. Rei is again the Crone, dispensing wisdom, but her Eva (which is also her ) is the most feared, the most feral, the most dangerous; Mother as Tiamat, woman as monster that despises men. In the series, avatars of the various aspects are raped or violated repeatedly, and sexual imagery is a frequent motif; the anatomy of the Evas is disturbingly feminine, as if summoned from the mind of a deranged Georgia O'Keeffe. Consider the Sachiel battle, with the angel's jackhammering pile driver attack. The masculine Angels constantly try to subdue and penetrate the Evas, and several of the battles, especially Bardiel and Armisael, are metaphors for the physical pain and loss of agency inherent in rape. Rei is subject

Article: Beyond the Monster Mother
 pile driver attack. The masculine Angels constantly try to subdue and penetrate the Evas, and several of the battles, especially Bardiel and Armisael, are metaphors for the physical pain and loss of agency inherent in rape. Rei is subject to constant sexual violence, beginning with Shinji's intrusion into her private space and groping, continuing through her violation by Armisael and culminating in Gendo's symbolic rape-as-mastery in EoE. The first is a fumbling, virginal event, as confusing to Shinji as it is merely annoying to Rei, tied to the idea of Maid. The second is a twisted vision of the sperm penetrating the egg. The third is Gendo violating her, not through the genitals but with his very Self(hand), leading to a violent reaction. She bites the hand that feeds her and takes on a war-aspect, becoming death. Asuka undergoes three trials, as well. She experiences three rapes, and three deaths. The first of these is her violation by Arael; her fist death is her suicide attempt by starvation, enacted in a bathroom. This is important- the bathroom in the apartment is the only place where Asuka has any privacy after she moves into the apartment. The bathroom where she finds her first death is blasted open, the privacy removed, her innocence destroyed by war and violence. Her second violation comes at the hands of the Mass Production Evas, creatures that are as terrifyingly masculine as Lilith is ovwhelmingly feminine. As toothed penises they represent a violent answer to the fear of vagina dentata. In their overwhelming numbers they represent sperm competing for an egg, but in a terrible inversion. Their licking and grasping and shoving of their phallic swords into Asuka-Kyoko is a violent, and disdainful gang rape, a ritual destruction of a virgin sacrifice to open the final act of the rite. Again, death finds her. The third violation comes in the kitchen in pre-Insturmentality. This event takes place not in Shinji's mind, but in Asuka's . The kitchen seen here is to Asuka as the hell train is to Shinji- her own personal purgatory, a place of scouring and a gentle hell. The hallmarks are all there. She is alone, which she fears more than anything, surrounded by symbols of her failed attempts at connection (she is in Misato's kitchen- despite her crowing about being able to live by herself and needing no

Article: Beyond the Monster Mother
 The hallmarks are all there. She is alone, which she fears more than anything, surrounded by symbols of her failed attempts at connection (she is in Misato's kitchen- despite her crowing about being able to live by herself and needing no one, she has no other place but the one she shared, and the coffee is not in a cup but a pot, implying sharing). The symbolic rape comes when Shinji forcefully enters and disrupts her hell, imposing his self and his bullshit problems on her. When she rejects him he replies with force in a symbolic rape murder that initiates her into apotheosis. Some other instances of rape imagery: The implied rape of Ritsuko as punishment. Misato's death by gut shot, identifying her with Rei's ascension. Note that following this, Misato's kiss, her first and only expression of physical affection towards him, leaves him bloody, linking it to a loss of virginity. The attempted rape-by-spear of of Yui-as-Eva. Fast forward to the ending of the rite. The end result is Shinji and Asuka on a beach. They have emerged from the ocean, as all life did. Each takes on aspects of the other. Shinji straddles her, taking on a feminine aspect, that of a woman in intercourse with a supine man, taking rather than receiving ; at the same time he enacts violence against her, crushing her throat (voice) with his hands (self) until she offers kindness (mirroring his mother's touch, motherly love and comfort) which ends his assault on her. The distinctions between them are no longer clear cut. The ritual is one of sexual violence. SEELE are the Illuminati, they are Them, They, the Conspiracy, the High Priesthood that has spent all of history erasing feminine identity and working to steal the aspect of life-giver from the mysteries of womanhood and transfer it to the male aspect, to identify man as seed and woman as vessel (see From Hell , Alan Moore) and to destroy femininity with sexual violence. In understanding its failure, we must consider a tripartite God, which also exists within Eva. The most immediately familiar expression of this notion is the Christian trinity, but that is useless here because it is not a true triple god. Rather, it is a ritualistic erasure of the importance of woman in the reproductive cycle. Child becomes Son, only Father retains his identity, and Mother, the secret consort of Jehova called

Article: Beyond the Monster Mother
 useless here because it is not a true triple god. Rather, it is a ritualistic erasure of the importance of woman in the reproductive cycle. Child becomes Son, only Father retains his identity, and Mother, the secret consort of Jehova called Asherah, is murdered leaving only a Holy Ghost. Rather, we need to consider the presence of male characters in the story- the most prominent one is Shinji, followed by Gendo, Fuyutsuki, Kaji, Hyuga, Aoba, Toji, and Kensuke. The others are background characters, there only to serve a plot purpose, and receive no development (although the admiral or whatever he is that appears in Asuka Strikes! is important as the bearded, Father God-esque hypermasculine commander of a feet of giant metal war penises traversing the ocean, the sacred font of life that is Tiamat the monster mother, but I digress) Shinji and the two boys at the school represent the Boy, equivalent of the Maid, the unblooded virgin. Toji is the stoicism of inexperience and childish seriousness, Kensuke the eagerness to pass through the rite of passage (hence his fixation on militaria and war, the playthings of the adult man and the defining aspect of adult masculinity in a tribal society organized for territorial warfare). Shinji combines aspects of both. He is at times feral and warlike and passive, terrified of Girls and entranced by their softness and comfort, the memory of his lost mother mingled with the growing need to sow his seed. When Shinji is removed from the equation this system of three, he is immediately replaced by Hikari, who herself embodies the goddess totality- she's virginal and inexperienced, yet acts as a mother (replacing her own absent one) and dispenses wisdom and acts as Queen within the community of the classroom, connecting her to the Crone. Within the room itself she is Mother to Asuka's Maid and Rei's Crone, as well. Gendo, Fuyutsuki, and Kaji represent aspects of the Father. Kaji is the Warm Father, the Boy's buddy. He's a cool dad who does things prefaced by "don't tell Mom", who guides the transition into adulthood with advice, comfort, and shared experience. As a spy archetype he seethes with violence and virility. He's Shinji's dad in the truest sense, much like Misato is his

Article: Beyond the Monster Mother
 tell Mom", who guides the transition into adulthood with advice, comfort, and shared experience. As a spy archetype he seethes with violence and virility. He's Shinji's dad in the truest sense, much like Misato is his mother. (It is important that Kaji dies, and Shinji symbolically takes virgin blood on his lip from Misato. This is a highly charged, Freudian moment where Shinji is truly initiated into the world of adulthood with a storm of sex and violence). He's the Adult Man as a warming companion who welcomes the Boy as a future colleague. He is the cool uncle that gives you a beer at New Years and pretends not to notice when you get into the old stack of playboys in his closet. Gendo is the Disciplinarian, the Cold Father, the aspect that the Boy resents, the aspect that he longs to kill to cement his possession of the mother, the figure that, through resentment and competition, promps the Boy to seize, rather than transition into, adulthood as an act of defiance. Fuyutsuki then is the Old Father. Distant, cold, emotionless, the aspect of adulthood that the Boy fears, being a transition from Father to Grandfather or Sage. He is the Father who wishes to change places, to steal the place of Boy and be young again, but is full of both regret and wisdom. SEELE themselves, embodied in Chairman Keel, are the Sage, the final aspect. Like many sages from myth, Keel is blind but has mystic sight and is a master of secrets. He is also King, a ruler, the power behind thrones. The goal of SEELE is to rewrite the entire dynamic, to create a unified being that is masculine without feminity, life without womb. This fails; womanhood is fluid like water and receives and transforms rather than is destroyed by masculine force. The seed of Adam implanted in Rei by Gendo does not overwhelm her. She seizes and gives it shape; the vessel wins over the seed. The end result is equilibrium. The new Adam cannot kill or assert over the new Lilith. Asuka who is now three-in-one achieves victory over him with kindness and births a new age in sight of the old goddess who has been sacrificed to herself. Asuka is ultimately the new Goddess. Repeatedly, the enemy attempted to debase, slay, and steal the sun-fire from her. Rei is the Goddess as Moon, mere reflection

Article: Beyond the Monster Mother
 of the old goddess who has been sacrificed to herself. Asuka is ultimately the new Goddess. Repeatedly, the enemy attempted to debase, slay, and steal the sun-fire from her. Rei is the Goddess as Moon, mere reflection of the Father Sun. Asuka in the new world is life giver, world maker, the Sun as Mother, womanhood ascendant.
